summary: Justin Brennan analyzed two multifamily deals live: a 12-unit value-add in Miami and a 20-unit stabilized property in Huntsville, Alabama. He walked through underwriting mechanics, cap rate analysis, renovation budgeting, and investor return structures using his deal analyzer tool.
takeaways: Use 5-10% vacancy assumption even on stabilized properties to stress-test and identify true market rents—when you hit that vacancy threshold, you know you're at market rent and running the business correctly
For value-add deals, aim to force appreciation by improving the cap rate 2 points from acquisition (e.g., buy at 6 cap, improve to 8 cap, then sell at 5.5-6 cap)
On stabilized properties with no major renovations, moving the cap rate up 1-2 points through operational improvements is sufficient
Use minimum 1.25 debt service coverage ratio as the hard floor—if numbers don't hit that, back into the price by reducing purchase offer until DCR works
For a 20-unit stabilized property, budget 6-8% blended management fee (5% base + leasing/commission fees), not the 5% broker's estimate
Set GP acquisition fee at 2-3% of purchase price, but confirm actual closing costs (title, points, lender fees) fit within total closing cost budget

Build investor returns with waterfall: preferred return (7% example) until met at 100%, then split equity 70/30 LP/GP; explain simply as annual cash flow percentage plus future appreciation at sale/refi
For renovation budgets on 12-unit 1-bed/studio: exterior work ~$50k, interior improvements ~$120k total (under $10k/unit with appliances ~$2k, flooring ~$2k), targeting $130+ monthly rent bumps
Stress-test deals by adjusting expense assumptions upward—if OM shows unusually low expenses, add back management fees and taxes to get realistic 40-50% expense ratio
Agency debt available for loans over $1M with 90%+ occupancy on 1980s-era product at 6.5% or lower rates

skill_candidates.change: Replace broker's expense assumptions with reality-tested figures (add management fees, realistic insurance/tax) to avoid overestimating NOI and cash-on-cash returns
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin identified 12-unit Miami deal had unrealistically low 81k expenses (33% ratio), corrected it to realistic 120k+ (45-50% ratio), and emphasized 'you'd normally see about 45-50%' rather than broker's optimistic number

skill_candidates.change: Systematize cap rate stress-testing by adjusting rates 0.25-0.5 points downward from broker guidance to model downside scenarios before offering
skill_candidates.evidence: On 20-unit Huntsville deal, Justin moved from broker's 5% to 5.5% cap, then tested at 6.75%, forcing price down from $1.75M guidance to $1.6M offer to maintain 1.25 DCR minimum

skill_candidates.change: Build renovation budgets by unit type and cost category (exterior/interior/per-unit), then validate rent bump potential matches the capex spend justification
skill_candidates.evidence: On Miami 12-unit, Justin prescribed $50k exterior + $120k interior ($10k/unit max with $2k appliances + $2k flooring breakdown) and tied it to $130+ rent bump to verify ROI; instructed 'you have to make sure you're going to get that' rent increase

skill_candidates.change: Write and send LOI tonight/before market visit rather than waiting; use soft whisper call to broker first at $575-600k to establish negotiation window before formal offer
skill_candidates.evidence: When Bruce said he was visiting Huntsville in 2.5 weeks, Justin said 'I'd write the offer like tonight if I were you' and coached: 'do a little whisper out to him before you send the LOI' to pretest $1.575-1.6M range rather than surprise broker

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A_05-27-25 | Justin Brennan
